title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination

Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.

The stacked area chart tracks India's merchandise exports as a share of GDP from 1949 to 2014, categorized by the continent of destination. The total height of the stacked areas represents the aggregate export share, which begins near 8% in 1949, declines to a low point below 4% around 1965, and then rises steadily to a peak exceeding 16% in the mid-2000s before settling near 16% in 2014. Exports to Asia, represented by the bottom blue band, constitute the largest component for most of the timeline, starting near 2%, dipping below 1% in the 1960s, and expanding significantly after 1990 to reach approximately 8% by the end of the period. The maroon band, indicating exports to Western Europe, remains a consistent layer, generally fluctuating between 1% and 3% throughout the decades. Exports to North America, shown in green, occupy a band roughly between 1% and 3% for the majority of the timeline, widening slightly in the later years. The upper layers, representing exports to South America, Africa, Eastern Europe, and Oceania, appear as thinner bands that collectively add between 1% and 4% to the total, with visible spikes in the Eastern Europe and Oceania categories around 2008. These estimates are derived from dyadic transactions data, where series such as "Western Europe" correspond to the sum of exports between all Western European countries divided by the GDP of Western Europe.
